title: C1-002 Failed-path ScanComplete makes the app render a FAILED scan as Completed
claim: The C1-002 fix added a terminal ScanComplete emission to the scan FAILED branch (scan_session.rs:573-588). But the Windows app's ScanCompleteEvent handler only suppresses the Phase overwrite for Cancelled, not Failed, so the new event immediately clobbers the just-emitted phaseChanged(Failed) with Phase=Completed — exactly the 'consumers must not infer completed from this event' outcome the fix's own comment warns against, and worse than the pre-fix behavior where the Failed phase stuck.
evidence: Engine (scan_session.rs:569-588): the Failed branch now does `emit_phase_guaranteed(&sink, SessionPhase::Failed).await;` then unconditionally `sink.send(ScanComplete{...}).await;`. Wire order is preserved (both are awaited sends). App consumer (platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/ViewModels/EngineClient.cs:1147-1181): `case ScanCompleteEvent sce:` guards only `if (Phase == ScanPhase.Cancelled) break;` (line 1163), then falls through to `Phase = ScanPhase.Completed;` (line 1167). The Phase property setter (EngineClient.cs:361 `private set => Set(ref _phase, value);`) has no monotonic clamp, so the assignment wins. Net: phaseChanged(Failed) sets Phase=Failed (and fires AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync at line 1132), then the new ScanComplete sets Phase=Completed. The pre-fix Failed branch emitted NO ScanComplete, so this overwrite is newly introduced.
suggested_fix: Either (a) do not emit ScanComplete on the engine's Failed branch (keep phaseChanged(Failed) as the sole terminal frame, as it was), or (b) add a Failed guard mirroring the Cancelled one in EngineClient.cs ScanCompleteEvent: `if (Phase == ScanPhase.Cancelled || Phase == ScanPhase.Failed) break;` so a Failed scan is not relabeled Completed.

title: Recycle Bin batch-restore matches paths case-SENSITIVELY, regressing a previously case-insensitive match
claim: The C1-007 rewrite of the per-item restore helper into restore_batch_from_recycle_bin replaced PowerShell's case-INSENSITIVE `-eq` path comparison with a .NET `HashSet[string]` default-comparer `.Contains()`, which is ordinal case-SENSITIVE. On Windows (case-insensitive filesystem) any case divergence between the bin's reconstructed `Join-Path $loc $i.Name` and the DB-stored original_path now fails to match, so the file is not restored and is reported as a failed restore even though it is recoverable.
evidence: Old helper (removed): `if ($loc -eq $wantParent -and $nm -eq $wantName)` — PowerShell `-eq` on strings is case-insensitive. New helper (trash.rs:244-254): `$wanted = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.HashSet[string];` (parameterless ctor → EqualityComparer<string>.Default → StringComparer.Ordinal, case-sensitive) then `if ($wanted.Contains($full))`. Divergence sources include drive-letter casing and Shell path normalization of System.Recycle.DeletedFrom vs the stored path_text. The caller treats a non-restored item as a failure (trash.rs:194-204), so a previously-working restore now silently fails.
suggested_fix: Create the set with an ordinal-ignore-case comparer: `New-Object System.Collections.Generic.HashSet[string]([System.StringComparer]::OrdinalIgnoreCase)` so the batch match restores the case-insensitivity the old `-eq` comparison had.

title: watch_parent_pinned can spuriously shut the engine down on a transient get_parent_pid() failure
claim: The F-C1-015 pinning rewrite re-samples get_parent_pid() after OpenProcess and signals shutdown if it does not equal the original ppid. get_parent_pid() uses CreateToolhelp32Snapshot, which can transiently fail (e.g. ERROR_BAD_LENGTH under process churn) and return None — making `still_parent` false and triggering an immediate engine shutdown while the parent is alive. The pre-fix watch_parent never re-sampled the PID, so this is a newly introduced spurious-shutdown path.
evidence: main.rs:542-551: `let pinned_creation = process_creation_time(handle); let still_parent = platform::get_parent_pid() == Some(ppid); if !still_parent || pinned_creation.is_none() { ... shutdown.notify_waiters(); return; }`. platform::get_parent_pid (platform.rs:806-829) returns None whenever CreateToolhelp32Snapshot or Process32FirstW/NextW errors. Because Windows stores a process's PPID at creation and never clears it, a live parent always yields still_parent=true EXCEPT when the snapshot transiently fails (None). The captured creation time is also only checked for is_none() and never compared, so the only realizable trigger of the shutdown branch is the transient snapshot failure. The old watch_parent (platform.rs:846+) only OpenProcess'd once and never called notify_waiters except on real parent death.
suggested_fix: Do not signal shutdown on a None get_parent_pid() re-sample: treat re-sample failure as inconclusive and fall back to platform::watch_parent (as the OpenProcess-failure branch already does) instead of notify_waiters(); only bail when the re-sample returns a concrete, different PID.

title: Revision-keyed sentinel rename invalidates every pre-existing Windows model install, forcing a full multi-GB re-download
claim: Changing the install sentinel name from `{id}.installed` to `{id}-{token}.installed` means the sentinel an already-installed Windows user has on disk can NEVER match the new name, so on the first launch after this update all models are reported as not-installed — blocking scans and forcing a full re-download of the (multi-GB) weights even though they are present and valid.
evidence: sentinel_path now returns `format!("{}-{}.installed", model.id, revision_token(model))` (registry.rs:571-575); the old form was a bare `{id}.installed`. All readers compare with `sentinel.exists()` and never glob: scan.rs:80-83 aborts the scan with `models_not_installed` if the sentinel is absent; prewarm.rs:244-257 only short-circuits when the exact path exists. prewarm then calls download_parallel (prewarm.rs:360-364), which has NO destination-exists/SHA fast-path (downloader.rs:591-616 goes straight to the HEAD probe and re-downloads), so the entire file is fetched again. The fix comment claims parity with a prior keyed scheme ("the old {id}-<oldtoken>.installed no longer matches"), but the Windows old name had no token at all, so this invalidates ALL installs, not just pin-bumped ones.
suggested_fix: On a missing revision-keyed sentinel, fall back to recognizing/migrating the legacy `{id}.installed` (treat it as valid for the current pin and rename it), or add a destination-exists+SHA-valid short-circuit in prewarm/download_parallel so a re-validate (not a re-download) heals the sentinel.

title: resume_seed_bytes double-counts oversized stale parts, letting the download progress bar exceed 100%
claim: For a resume where a `.part-NN` file is LARGER than its planned range (the documented different-sized-remote / pin-bump case), the new seed counts `range_len` for that part, but download_range_with_retry then discards the entire oversized part and re-downloads the full range, emitting `range_len` of progress deltas — so that part is counted twice and bytes_done overshoots bytes_total.
evidence: resume_seed_bytes does `seed += on_disk.min(range_len)` (downloader.rs:877), so an oversized part contributes range_len to the seed. But download_range_with_retry treats an oversized part as fully stale: `if existing_len > range_len { remove_file; existing_len = 0; }` (downloader.rs:954-960), then re-fetches from cur_start=start and sends a delta for every byte (downloader.rs:1065). Net for that part = range_len (seed) + range_len (re-download deltas) = 2x. The fix comment and the test both assert the wrong model ("discards the overflow" / clamps to range_len) when the code discards the WHOLE part, so the in-range prefix does not actually survive.
suggested_fix: Only seed an oversized part if it would be reused; since an oversized part is fully discarded, contribute 0 for it: `seed += if on_disk > range_len { 0 } else { on_disk };` (matching the existing_len>range_len discard in download_range_with_retry).

title: Home-anchor redaction can over-redact non-home paths, collapsing a path (incl. filename) to "…"
claim: The new home-anchored masking finds the FIRST component literally named `users`/`home` and unconditionally treats the next component as the username. When such a directory is itself the second-to-last component, the code masks the FILENAME as if it were the username, dropping useful debug context — in the worst case returning just "…".
evidence: platform.rs:70-88: home_anchor = first `users`/`home` position; user_idx=anchor+1; the branch fires when `parts.len() - user_idx <= 2`. For e.g. `E:\Backups\Users\file.txt` → parts=[Backups,Users,file.txt], anchor=1, user_idx=2 points at `file.txt`, `after=parts[3..]` is empty, so `kept.is_empty()` → returns "…" (line 83-84), losing the filename. The previous generic-tail code returned "…/Users/file.txt". Privacy-safe (never leaks more than before — the new branch always keeps a subset of the old tail), but it degrades logging for any path with a `users`/`home` directory as the 2nd/3rd-from-last component (backup trees, NAS shares, etc.).
suggested_fix: Only treat parts[anchor+1] as a username when there is at least one component after it (i.e. it is not the file itself): require `parts.len() - user_idx >= 2` AND the anchor be a plausible home root, or fall through to the generic tail when `after` is empty so the filename is preserved.

title: Manual face clustering becomes a silent no-op after any scan cancel (sticky cancel mirror)
claim: The F-C3-042 cancellation fix wires clustering to the scan-scoped, sticky ScanCoordinator cancel mirror, so a manually-triggered clustering pass aborts immediately and persists nothing after any prior scan cancellation.
evidence: runClustering now calls IdentityClustering.cluster(shouldCancel: { ScanCoordinator.isCancelledSync() }) (FaceClustering.swift:210) and returns early when icResult.cancelled is true (FaceClustering.swift:215-221). ScanCoordinator.cancelMirror is set TRUE in requestCancel() (ScanCoordinator.swift:134), which is invoked on the .cancelScan and .shutdown commands (FileIDEngineMain.swift:217,229). The mirror is reset to FALSE in exactly one place: startSession() (ScanCoordinator.swift:81). The standalone .runFaceClustering IPC command (People-tab "Cluster faces", FileIDEngineMain.swift:230-258) and autoEnqueue both call runClustering WITHOUT starting a scan session. So after a user cancels a scan (a common action) in the same long-lived engine process, cancelMirror stays true; the next manual cluster hits shouldCancel() on the very first Pass-1 poll (IdentityClustering.swift:148), returns cancelled, and runClustering returns before the persist transaction (line 288). It still emits .faceClusteringComplete with personCount:0, so the People tab shows 'clustering done, 0 persons' with no error and no clusters written, until a fresh scan resets the mirror. Pre-fix, runClustering had no shouldCancel and always ran to completion regardless of cancelMirror.
suggested_fix: Do not gate the standalone/auto clustering job on the scan-cancel mirror. Either clear the mirror at the start of a clustering job, or give clustering its own cancellation token scoped to the faceCluster JobQueue job (set on shutdown only), so a previously-cancelled scan can't suppress a later manual cluster.

title: Discovery skip set defeats the orphan sweep on incremental rescans (deleted files no longer pruned)
claim: The new F-C6-001 skip set (Discovery.swift) interacts with the unchanged orphan sweep (FileIDEngineMain.sweepOrphans) so that, on a non-forced rescan of a large library, genuinely-deleted files are no longer removed from the DB.
evidence: enumerate() now `continue`s on skipped files (lines 198-204), so they are never emitted to the DBWriter. The DBWriter UPSERT is the only place `scanned_at` is bumped (DBWriter.swift:522,542 — it runs for unchanged emitted files too, BEFORE the `if unchanged { return }` at line 569). Pre-campaign, discovery emitted every file, so after a rescan all present files had `scanned_at >= scanStart` and the orphan-sweep candidate query `WHERE ... AND scanned_at < ?` (FileIDEngineMain.swift:736-744) returned only truly-stale rows — a small set. After F-C6-001, every skipped (unchanged, failed=0) file retains the PRIOR scan's `scanned_at`, which is `< scanStart`, so ALL of them become candidates. The candidate query has no ORDER BY and a `LIMIT 5000` (line 738); on a library of tens of thousands of files the cap is saturated by present-on-disk skipped rows, and the fileExists() gate (line 758-759) only deletes the misses among those 5000. Real orphans outside the arbitrary first-5000 rowids are never stat'd and never deleted.
suggested_fix: On a skip, still bump the row's `scanned_at` to the current scan time (a cheap UPDATE-only path, or feed skipped paths to the writer as a 'touch'), OR drop the `scanned_at < scanStart` pre-filter in sweepOrphans and rely solely on fileExists() (raising/removing the 5000 cap or paging), so the skip set and the orphan sweep agree on what 'seen this scan' means.

title: Incremental skip predicate is looser than the DBWriter 'unchanged' check (backdated-mtime same-size edits skipped forever)
claim: Discovery's skip test (`scanned_at >= mtime` + size match) is weaker than the established change-detection in DBWriter (`stored mtime == current mtime` + size match), so a file whose mtime moved to a value still <= the last scan time but != its stored mtime is skipped and never re-tagged, even though DBWriter would have reprocessed it.
evidence: isAlreadyCurrent (lines 243-251) returns true when `dbScannedAt >= modified && dbSize == currentSize`. DBWriter.unchanged (DBWriter.swift:482-494) instead requires `abs(oldModified - newModified) < 1e-6`. Consider a file stored with modified_at=M1, scanned_at=T1 (T1>M1); later its mtime is backdated by an archive extract / `rsync -a` / git checkout / Time Machine restore to M2 with M1 != M2 but M2 <= T1, while its byte size is unchanged. Discovery: T1 >= M2 => SKIP. DBWriter (had it been reached): M1 != M2 => reprocess. Because discovery drops the file upstream, the new content is never re-tagged/OCR'd and stale tags/faces/OCR persist.
suggested_fix: Make the skip predicate match DBWriter's contract: skip only when the file's current mtime EQUALS the stored modified_at (and size matches), i.e. load modified_at into SkipEntry and compare equality, rather than comparing scanned_at >= mtime.

title: Bare st_ino used as heal identity — APFS inode reuse can re-bind a deleted file's row onto an unrelated new file
claim: Discovery.inode() supplies a raw APFS/HFS st_ino as the cross-platform 'fileRef', but the DBWriter rename/move heal trusts file_ref equality alone. Unlike the Windows NTFS file_ref (which carries a 16-bit sequence/generation number that invalidates a stale reference), st_ino is freely reused after deletion, so the heal can mis-rebind.
evidence: inode() (lines 229-241) returns `UInt64(st.st_ino)`. healMovedRow (DBWriter.swift:704-734) re-binds any row whose `file_ref` equals the new file's and whose old path is GONE — with no size/mtime/content corroboration. Sequence: file A (inode N, with id/created_at/tags/manual person_id) is deleted; APFS later assigns inode N to a brand-new file B at a new path. On rescan B is discovered with fileRef=N, existing==nil at B's path triggers heal (DBWriter.swift:455-459), A's old path is gone, so A's row (and its id/created_at, and FK children where re-detection doesn't overwrite them) is re-bound to B. This window is widened by finding #1: A's orphan row now lingers in the DB, keeping its file_ref available to match a reused inode.
suggested_fix: Corroborate the heal with at least one additional signal that survives a move but differs across distinct files (size, and/or content_hash if available), or gate macOS heal more conservatively; document that st_ino lacks the NTFS sequence-number reuse protection the Windows path relies on.

title: Shared single-flight loadTask: cancelPrewarm()/requestCancel() collaterally cancel a concurrent same-model run/prewarm
claim: Because F-C3-023 makes a prewarm and a Deep Analyze run share ONE unstructured `loadTask`, and F-C3-024 wires both cancellers to cancel it bluntly, cancelling a prewarm aborts a concurrent run that joined the same load (and vice-versa).
evidence: loadTask is shared (line 44). A real run joins an in-flight prewarm load via ensureLoaded's join branch (lines 130-149) → awaitLoad. cancelPrewarm() calls `loadTask?.cancel()` (line 71) and requestCancel() calls `loadTask?.cancel()` (line 62). Prewarm runs outside JobQueue (FileIDEngineMain.swift:369) so it genuinely overlaps a run. If `.cancelPrewarm` arrives while a run is joined to the prewarm's download, the run's awaitLoad receives CancellationError and DeepAnalyzeRunner.run treats it as cancelled-during-load (DeepAnalyzeRunner.swift around the cold-load catch), emitting deepAnalyzeComplete(cancelled:true) and doing zero analysis — even though the user only cancelled the prewarm. The reverse (deepAnalyzeCancel killing a concurrent prewarm install) holds symmetrically.
suggested_fix: Reference-count or scope cancellation: only cancel the shared loadTask when the LAST waiter cancels, or keep separate per-caller cancellation so a prewarm cancel doesn't abort a joined run's load (and vice-versa).

title: Negated-same heuristic can flip an explicit affirmative 'VERDICT: SAME' to DIFFERENT
claim: containsNegatedSame matches 'NOT/CANNOT/…N'T [<=2 words] SAME' anywhere in the reply, so a model that returns an explicit affirmative verdict plus incidental free text containing a negated 'same' now parses as DIFFERENT.
evidence: parseFaceComparison sets `saidSame = !negatedSame && (...VERDICT: SAME...)` (lines 386-390). A reply like 'VERDICT: SAME\nCONFIDENCE: 0.92\nThese are not in the same lighting but clearly the same person.' contains 'NOT IN THE SAME' (NOT + 2 words + SAME), so containsNegatedSame (lines 422-435) returns true and the explicit SAME verdict is discarded → sameClass=false. Impact is a MISSED auto-merge (safe direction — never a wrong merge), but it is a behavior change vs the pre-fix parser, which returned SAME for that input.
suggested_fix: Only apply the negated-same override when no explicit 'VERDICT: SAME' line is present (i.e., gate negatedSame to the loose `!saidDifferent && contains("SAME")` branch, not the explicit-verdict branch).

title: loadImageAndEXIF now trusts discovery's sizeBytes; an absent fileSizeKey marks a valid image as decode-failed
claim: Replacing the per-file attributesOfItem stat with the discovered sizeBytes means a regular file whose `.fileSizeKey` failed to populate at discovery (size becomes 0) is now treated as <256 B and skipped as corrupt, instead of being decoded.
evidence: loadImageAndEXIF now does `if sizeBytes < 256 { return nil }` (Tagging.swift:344) using discovered.sizeBytes. Discovery computes `let size = Int64(values?.fileSize ?? 0)` (Discovery.swift:186) — if resourceValues returns a non-nil value set with isRegularFile present but fileSize absent (possible on some SMB/NFS volumes, which this project targets heavily), size is 0. The old code re-stat'd via attributesOfItem and got the true size, so it would still decode. Now the image yields image_decode_failed. (It is marked failed=true, so it retries next scan, limiting the blast radius.)
suggested_fix: Treat a 0/absent discovered size as 'unknown' rather than 'tiny': fall back to a stat (or skip the <256 guard) when sizeBytes <= 0 instead of short-circuiting to decode-failed.

title: Discovery incremental-skip (F-C6-001) makes DBWriter's CLIP-backfill-on-unchanged path unreachable — post-install rescans never backfill embeddings
claim: The F-C6-001 wave wired discovery to drop unchanged files before the pipeline (FileIDEngineMain.swift:568-571), but the skip predicate keys only on size + scanned_at, not on whether a CLIP embedding exists. This renders DBWriter's documented 'CLIP model installed after the original scan -> backfill the embedding' branch (DBWriter.swift:569-578) effectively dead code, so embeddings are no longer backfilled on a normal incremental rescan.
evidence: DBWriter.swift:569 `if unchanged { if let blob = file.clipEmbeddingBlob { ... if !hasEmbedding { try insertClipEmbedding(...) } } ; return }` only runs if the file reaches insertOne. FileIDEngineMain.swift:568 now calls `discovery.walk(root: url, database: database, forceReprocess: rescan, ...)`. Discovery.buildSkipSet (Discovery.swift:275-278) loads `SELECT path_text, size_bytes, scanned_at FROM files WHERE failed = 0 ...` and isCurrent (Discovery.swift:248-249) skips when `size unchanged AND scanned_at >= modified` — no clip_embeddings check. On main, walk had no database param, so every file reached insertOne and the backfill branch worked. The branch's only reachable case (forceReprocess=false AND size+mtime match) is exactly the set discovery now filters out (a forced rescan instead sets unchanged=false, taking the full-rebuild branch, not the cheap backfill).
suggested_fix: Either (a) exclude files lacking a clip_embeddings row from the discovery skip set (LEFT JOIN clip_embeddings / NOT EXISTS) so an embeddable image without an embedding is still processed, or (b) trigger a forced/targeted re-embed pass after a CLIP model is installed. Update the DBWriter comment to reflect whichever mechanism backfills embeddings.

title: IPCSink pins restructureApplyResult but not restructurePlan — the new planRestructure terminal reply is not protected from eviction
claim: The campaign's terminal-event pinning fix (F-C3-029/030) added restructureApplyResult to the pinned/critical set but omitted restructurePlan, even though restructurePlan is the success-path terminal reply for the brand-new planRestructure flow (FileIDEngineMain.swift:432-447). The app's 'Computing plan…' state recovers only on restructurePlan or the plan_restructure_failed error — and the error path IS pinned (error is critical) while the success path is not, an asymmetry where a failed plan is always delivered but a successful plan can be evicted.
evidence: isCritical (IPCSink.swift:194-205) lists `.scanComplete, .deepAnalyzeComplete, .faceClusteringComplete, .restructureApplyResult, .error, .ready, .discoveryComplete, .phaseChanged` — no `.restructurePlan`. criticalNeedles (IPCSink.swift:211-220) likewise omits `"restructurePlan"`. planRestructure emits `.restructurePlan(plan)` via sink.emit (FileIDEngineMain.swift:439). Under a full buffer of all-critical entries, the eviction path drops a non-critical newcomer (`else if !Self.isCritical(payload) { return }`, IPCSink.swift:86-89), which would silently drop restructurePlan and strand the Restructure tab.
suggested_fix: Add `.restructurePlan` to isCritical and `Data("\"restructurePlan\"".utf8)` to criticalNeedles, matching the stated 'every terminal completion is pinned' invariant. Trigger is narrow (buffer must be entirely pinned events), so low severity, but it is a consistency gap within the same fix.

title: Tag-undo consumer ignores the new `skipped` count from undoBulkAdd (cross-wave gap)
claim: The F-C3-034 fix changed TagWriter.undoBulkAdd to return a 4-tuple with a new `skipped` count and to skip entries that fail the age/identity guard, but the (unchanged) app consumer undoLastTagBatch never consumes `skipped`: it clears the journal on `result.failed == 0` and omits skipped from the status message — diverging from the parallel rename-undo path which it should mirror.
evidence: TagWriter.undoBulkAdd (TagWriter.swift:243-285) now returns (undone, skipped, failed, firstError) and increments `skipped` for age-expired (>7d) or identity-mismatched entries. LibraryView.undoLastTagBatch (LibraryView.swift:577-582) does `if result.failed == 0 { BulkTagSheet.clearLastBatch() }` and builds undoStatus from undone+failed+firstError only. The sibling rename path undoLastBatch (LibraryView.swift:559-563) instead clears only when `result.failed == 0 && result.skipped == 0` and appends `· skipped N`. So after a stale/replaced-file tag batch where everything is skipped (undone=0, skipped=N, failed=0), the journal is cleared and the user sees 'Removed tags from 0 files' with no explanation and no ability to retry.
suggested_fix: Mirror the rename path: gate the clear on `result.failed == 0 && result.skipped == 0` and append `· skipped \(result.skipped)` to undoStatus when skipped > 0.

title: checkSizePlausible can delete a SHA256-verified file (size gate runs even after a passing hash)
claim: The new size-plausibility gate is documented as the 'no-hash case' fallback, but it runs unconditionally — after a successful expectedSHA256 verification. With a passing hash, a too-high `approxBytes` estimate (>4x actual) makes checkSizePlausible throw and delete the just-downloaded, integrity-verified file, causing an unrecoverable repeated failure for that model.
evidence: In streamingDownload, the SHA check at lines 75-90 is followed by `try checkSizePlausible(actual: actualSize, approxBytes: approxBytes)` at line 97 (catch removes tempURL, line 99). Same pattern in parallelStreamingDownload: SHA at 331-339, then checkSizePlausible at line 345 (catch removes finalTemp, 347). checkSizePlausible rejects when `actual < approxBytes/4` (line 480-482). Callers pass both together — e.g. ArcFaceModelInstaller.swift:113-115 passes `approxBytes: kind.approxBytes` and `expectedSHA256: ModelManifest.sha256(...)`. When SHA matches, the file is provably correct; the size gate can then only false-reject, never catch a real problem. Current manifest approxBytes are accurate so it does not misfire today, but it is a latent delete-verified-file path.
suggested_fix: Skip the size gate when a hash was verified: only call checkSizePlausible when `expectedSHA256 == nil` (matching the comment's stated 'no-hash case' intent).

title: preflightDiskSpace over-estimates need (2x) on the single-stream path
claim: preflightDiskSpace requires 2x approxBytes free on the destination volume, with a comment justifying the 2x by the parallel path's parts+concat staging. But it is also invoked from the single-stream streamingDownload (line 58), where the file transits the system temp dir and only needs ~1x on the destination volume; in the large-file parallel->single fallback with free space between 1x and 2x, this can falsely reject a download that would otherwise succeed.
evidence: preflightDiskSpace (lines 461-468) computes `needed = approxBytes * 2` and is called at streamingDownload line 58 and parallelStreamingDownload line 191. The single-stream path moves through FileManager.temporaryDirectory (delegate didFinishDownloadingTo, lines 786-789) then moveAtomically to dest (line 104), so peak destination-volume usage is ~1x, not 2x. The 2x reasoning in the comment (lines 455-460) applies only to the parallel staging+concat path.
suggested_fix: Parameterize the multiplier (2x for parallel, 1x for single-stream), or only call preflightDiskSpace from the parallel path / pass a `peakMultiplier`.

title: Library tile tag-dots stop refreshing for in-app tag edits during a live scan
claim: The new scan-frozen `tileRefreshToken` keeps each FileTile's `.task(id:)` pinned to a frozen version during a scan, so a Finder-tag edit / bulk-tag / bulk-undo performed in-app while a scan is running does not refresh the tile's tag dots until the scan completes.
evidence: `tileRefreshToken` (lines 74-76) returns `scanActive ? frozenTileVersion : store.version`, and `frozenTileVersion` is only updated `.onChange(of: store.version){ if !scanActive { frozenTileVersion = v } }` (lines 164-166). FileTile keys on it via `.task(id: "\(row.id)·\(refreshToken)")` (line 896). Previously the tile keyed directly on `store.version`, so an in-app tag edit (which calls store.notifyChanged() → version bump) re-tasked the tile and re-read the Finder xattrs. The freeze's justifying comment ("Finder xattrs can't change while the engine only writes DB rows") overlooks that the app itself writes xattrs (FinderTagsEditor / BulkTagSheet) during a scan, so those edits now render stale until the scan's phase leaves discovering/tagging/postScan.
suggested_fix: Track a separate, edit-only epoch (bumped by tag/undo writes via notifyChanged) that is NOT frozen during a scan, and combine it with the frozen counter epoch in the tile id; or invalidate just the edited file's tile. Cosmetic and self-heals at scan end, so low priority.

title: EngineClient.start() blocks the @MainActor on proc.waitUntilExit() with no timeout
claim: start() now calls terminateRunningEngine() which does proc.terminate() + proc.waitUntilExit() synchronously; because EngineClient is @MainActor, this blocks the UI thread until the old engine dies, with no timeout — a 'Restart Engine' against a busy/wedged engine can stall (or, if SIGTERM is ever delayed, hang) the UI.
evidence: EngineClient is `@MainActor` (line 7). start() calls `terminateRunningEngine()` first (line 137); terminateRunningEngine does `proc.terminate(); proc.waitUntilExit()` (lines 167-168) on the calling (main) actor. waitUntilExit() has no timeout. The previous start() did not terminate the prior process at all, so this synchronous external-process wait on the UI thread is newly introduced. The engine only installs `signal(SIGPIPE, SIG_IGN)` (no SIGTERM handler), so SIGTERM default-terminates and the stall is usually brief, but a process stuck in an uninterruptible state would block the UI indefinitely.
suggested_fix: Bound the wait (poll isRunning with a timeout, then escalate to SIGKILL), or perform the terminate+reap off the main actor and await it before spawning, so the UI thread is never blocked on an unbounded external wait.

title: RestructureView 'Computing…' spinner has incomplete failure-recovery paths (wedges on oversize plan and on repeated identical errors)
claim: After routing Restructure through the engine butler (F-C3-021-app), the only automatic clear of the `loading` spinner on a failed plan is `.onChange(of: engine.lastError?.message)` gated to kinds plan_restructure_failed/db_unavailable. Two distinct triggers leave `loading=true` forever: (a) a plan frame over the 32 MiB inbound cap is dropped with kind `ipc_frame_too_large`, which the guard ignores; (b) two consecutive plan failures producing the identical message string do not re-fire onChange, so a retry after the same error never clears the spinner.
evidence: RestructureView lines 290-294: `.onChange(of: engine.lastError?.message){ guard loading, let kind = engine.lastError?.kind, kind == "plan_restructure_failed" || kind == "db_unavailable" else { return }; loading = false; ... }`. requestPlan() sets loading=true but does not clear engine.lastError, so a repeated identical failure does not change `.message` and onChange does not fire. Separately, EngineClient.spawn() drops oversize frames and sets `lastError = EngineError(kind: "ipc_frame_too_large", ...)` (EngineClient.swift ~line 309); the engine emits the full plan as one un-length-capped JSON line (the campaign raised the cap from 1 MiB to 32 MiB precisely because butler plans are large), so a very large library's plan can exceed 32 MiB, be dropped, and leave the tab stuck on 'Computing…' with no plan and no spinner reset (kind not handled).
suggested_fix: In the loading-recovery onChange, also treat kind == "ipc_frame_too_large" as a terminal plan failure (clear loading + show a 'plan too large' message), and key recovery on lastError identity/kind rather than only the message string (or call engine.clearLastError() in requestPlan so a repeated error is a real change). Recoverable today only via Restart Engine or re-picking a folder.

title: Post-apply re-plan catch is dead code — single-flight guard can stick, exception silently swallowed
claim: The F-C5-003 single-flight fix releases `_applying` in SyncApplyResult's success path by firing a fire-and-forget re-plan wrapped in a try/catch, but that catch can never observe the failure it was written to handle, so a re-plan send fault leaves the Apply buttons permanently disabled with the error silently dropped.
evidence: SyncApplyResult (line 680): `try { _ = EngineClient.Instance.PlanRestructureAsync(folder!); } catch (Exception ex) { DebugLog.Warn(...); _applying = false; RecomputeSelection(); }`. When `r.Applied > 0` and folder is non-empty (the normal success case) the code intentionally KEEPS `_applying = true` and relies on either (a) SyncPlan releasing it when the new plan arrives, or (b) this catch releasing it if the re-plan throws. But `PlanRestructureAsync` is `=> SendCommandAsync(...)` (EngineClient.Commands.cs:658), and SendCommandAsync NEVER throws synchronously — frame-too-large and not-Ready return `Task.FromException(...)` (Commands.cs:39,50) and the pipe write runs inside `Task.Run` (Commands.cs:56) so IOExceptions fault the returned Task. The result is discarded with `_ =`, so the sync-only `catch` cannot fire for any of these. Consequently, if the re-plan send faults (engine transitioned out of Ready, or stdin/pipe died in the window right after the apply result), no new plan arrives (SyncPlan never runs) and no engine-side LastError arrives (SyncEngineError only fires when the engine RECEIVES and rejects a command), so `_applying` stays true forever: ApplySymlinkButton/ApplyMovesButton remain disabled (set false at lines 639-640 / RecomputeSelection lines 607-608) with no recovery path until the user navigates away or manually triggers a fresh plan, and the underlying exception is unobserved (the DebugLog.Warn at 683 never executes). Contrast ApplyAsync (line 646) which correctly `await`s ApplyRestructureAsync inside its try/catch and so does release the guard on a send fault (lines 654-655).
suggested_fix: Observe the re-plan Task instead of discarding it: make the release/log fire on the faulted task, e.g. `_ = EngineClient.Instance.PlanRestructureAsync(folder!).ContinueWith(t => DispatcherQueue.TryEnqueue(() => { DebugLog.Warn("Restructure post-apply re-plan failed: " + t.Exception?.GetBaseException().Message); _applying = false; RecomputeSelection(); }), TaskContinuationOptions.OnlyOnFaulted);` (or await it in an async helper). The current synchronous try/catch around a discarded Task can never run.
